serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one glass of approximately 200 ml, providing around 46 kcal and 8.8 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Tomato juice drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for tomato juice drink (cr_id D03070036). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, vitamin C, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB for this entry are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 33 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for tomato juice (unsweetened). This tomato juice drink contains added sweeteners which may marginally raise GI; however, the carbohydrate content (4.38 g/100g) remains low and the GI is conservatively estimated at 33 (low category). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (33 × 8.76 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.9 (very low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 173 mg/100 g — borderline for a beverage; commercial variants may contain 300–500 mg/100 g due to added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should choose 'no added salt' tomato juice drink variants and verify the sodium content on the nutrition label, as commercial formulations frequently contain significantly higher sodium than the generic value of 173 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.38 g/100 g), low sugar (3.46 g/100 g), and low GI (~33) support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tomato juice drink has a low glycaemic index (~33) and low carbohydrate load, making it a suitable beverage choice for people with type 2 diabetes when consumed in standard portions; however, sweetened commercial variants should be checked for added sugars on the label.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 173 mg/100 g is a concern for CVD; fat is negligible (0.07 g/100 g) and the beverage provides lycopene antioxidants from tomato
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The very low fat content and lycopene from tomato are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the sodium content (173 mg/100 g in the generic form, potentially higher in commercial products) warrants label checking; individuals with CVD should select low-sodium or no-added-salt variants.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade-derived beverage; solanine alkaloids in tomato may trigger inflammatory responses in RA-susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tomato juice drink is a nightshade-derived beverage and should be consumed cautiously by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade alkaloids; if tolerated, the lycopene and vitamin C content may offer antioxidant benefit, but high-sodium variants should be avoided as excess sodium can worsen tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato juice drink is a processed beverage made from reconstituted tomato juice concentrate, water, and sweeteners. It is commercially produced and widely available in Singapore and globally. The drink has a mild, slightly sweet tomato flavour with a thin, watery consistency compared to pure tomato juice. Per 100 g it provides 23 kcal, 0.83 g protein, 0.07 g fat, 4.38 g carbohydrate (of which 3.46 g is sugar), 0.52 g dietary fibre, and 173 mg sodium. Calcium (18 mg/100 g) and iron (0.58 mg/100 g) are present in modest amounts. The glycaemic index is approximately 33 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes when consumed in standard portions. The sodium content of 173 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management; commercial variants may contain significantly more sodium due to added salt, so label checking is advised. The beverage is very low in fat and calories, making it appropriate for low-calorie dietary patterns. Tomato is a nightshade plant, and individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to solanine alkaloids should monitor their response. The lycopene content from tomato provides antioxidant properties relevant to cardiovascular and cancer-risk contexts. This record is sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo food composition database (cr_id D03070036).

confidence_reason: Input record carries HPB Foodo provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore food composition database entry for tomato juice drink (cr_id D03070036). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for tomato juice (closest approved analogous source).
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB for this entry): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for tomato juice drink (D03070036) as of the access date. The input dietary_tags included 'keto'; this is retained in cultural context but is borderline — at 4.38 g carbohydrate/100 g the drink is low-carb but a 200 ml serving contributes ~8.8 g carbohydrate which may approach keto limits depending on total daily intake. Sodium value of 173 mg/100 g is the generic HPB value; commercial tomato juice drink products may contain 300–500 mg sodium/100 g due to added salt — consumers should verify on product labels. GI of 33 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for tomato juice; the addition of sweeteners in this drink formulation may marginally increase GI but the overall carbohydrate load remains low.
